The Manifesto — Pinned To The Wall, Zürich
P. 002 / 064

A short, opinionated theory of

WritingAs ThinkingMade Visible.
Drafted ByM. Reinhardt & The Bureau
EditionThird — Spring 2025
Read InTwelve Quiet Minutes
§ 01.The Premise
W

e have spent ten years watching writing tools mistake speed for help. They finish our sentences before we have finished thinking them, predict the word the room expects, and call it intelligence. We disagree, politely.

Writing is not the typing. Writing is the small, structural act of choosing a sentence over the sentence next to it — an act that requires the writer to be slightly uncomfortable, slightly slower than the machine. Prose & Co. was built to protect that discomfort, not to relieve it.

"We do not finish your sentence. We hand it back to you, slightly heavier than it was."

This is a manifesto, in the old sense: a short list of beliefs we are willing to be unfashionable about. There are five of them. They are written in plain English so that we cannot wriggle out of any of them later.

Marginalia — Note 1

"Speed is a virtue in messengers, not in editors." — pinned above M. Reinhardt's desk, on a strip of masking tape.

An Honest Number
71%

Of our subscribers told us, unprompted, that the Editor "does less than I expected, on purpose." We took it as a compliment.

Marginalia — Note 2

If the room expects the word, the Editor will quietly suggest the second-best one. Surprise is a feature.

§ 02. — Five Tenets, Plainly Stated
What We Believe.

Pin them up. Argue with us. Write back if any of them are wrong — we will print the letter.

01

The Sentence Is The Unit.

Not the paragraph. Not the document. The Editor is built around the sentence because that is where thinking actually happens — one decision at a time, one line at a time.

02

Suggest, Never Substitute.

We will never quietly replace your words with ours. The Editor proposes, in the margin, the way a real editor does. The author keeps the pen.

03

Cadence Over Correctness.

Grammar is a floor, not a ceiling. We will defend a comma splice if it earns its keep, and we will argue against a perfectly correct sentence if it bores us both.

04

Sources, Visible. Always.

Every fact carries its citation. Every guess is labelled a guess. If the Editor is uncertain, it says so, in a tone of voice that does not pretend otherwise.

05

The Writer's Voice Is The Product.

We do not train on your drafts. We do not flatten you into the mean. The house style we offer is one option, not a verdict — and yours is always the one we are protecting.

§ 03. — Apophatic Definitions
What We Will Not Do.

A manifesto is also a list of refusals. Some of these are technically possible. We have chosen not to ship them, and we would like the choice to be on the record.

01 .

We will not autocomplete in the body of the document.

Ghost text trains writers to accept the obvious word. We refuse to be a worse version of your own voice.

02 .

We will not score your writing out of one hundred.

There is no such number. Any product that supplies one is selling theatre.

03 .

We will not train on customer drafts.

Ever. Not anonymised, not aggregated, not “to improve the service.” Your sentences are yours.

04 .

We will not bury the model behind a personality.

No mascots, no first-name AI, no "hello, friend!" The Editor speaks in the voice of an editor: brief, particular, slightly tired.

05 .

We will not chase the leaderboard.

We optimise for one number: the percentage of suggestions the writer actually accepts and keeps in the final draft. Nothing else.

§ 04. — In Closing
If You Have ReadThis Far, You AreAlready One Of Us.

Fourteen days on the house. No card, no asterisks. If, on day fifteen, you decide we are wrong about all of this — you owe us nothing but the favour of writing back to tell us why.

Sign The ManifestoWrite To The Bureau
— M. ReinhardtEditor-At-Large — Zürich, March 2025
Prose & Co.

A writing studio with an opinion. Drafted in Zürich, edited in Kyoto, printed nowhere.
